wkr-007-001.summary: Documentation correctly frames the task-claim atomic-rename pattern, but the metaswarm collab ledgers lean on bare O_APPEND with multi-KB JSON payloads written by up to ~50 concurrent commanders, and lack the per-writer line-buffering, size cap, flock, or fsync needed to make that guarantee real.
top_issues: Multi-writer collab ledgers (proposals/reviews/etc) may corrupt when payload > PIPE_BUF
child-agents.jsonl is single-writer (safe today) but undocumented as such
No fsync anywhere — partial trailing lines after crash tolerated not prevented

wkr-007-002.summary: Collab ledger line counts are safe when read by the monitor, but persisted collab.counts are initialized to zero and never maintained. Swarm-state telemetry counts lack a locking/atomic update mechanism so concurrent writers can lose updates.
top_issues: collab.counts initialized to 0 and never updated — always stale
swarm-state.json telemetry counts have no locking — lost updates possible
mark_run_blocked() can overwrite concurrent commander telemetry updates

wkr-007-003.summary: The adoption workflow has three install paths but only git clone + install.sh produces a working end-to-end installation. The quickstart.sh hero path installs only the orchestrator skill without agents, shell scripts, or prerequisite checks, meaning a new users first experience will fail.
top_issues: quickstart.sh hero path produces incomplete install (no agents/scripts)
Missing prerequisite checks in both quickstart.sh and install.sh
Contradictory max agent counts across docs (8 vs 20)
Metaswarm not discoverable — buried in docs

wkr-007-004.summary: broadcasts.jsonl is not actually consumed by orchestrator synthesis; consumption is only requested in the commander launch prompt. Validation, dedupe, cursor tracking, and provenance reporting are not concretely implemented.
top_issues: No cursor/pointer to track broadcast read position
No deduplication — same broadcast could be consumed twice
No schema validation before broadcast consumption

wkr-007-005.summary: Stampede preserves ledger durability well: teardown/kill-session leave .stampede/{run_id}/ intact with all .jsonl files. Recovery logic re-queues claimed tasks from filesystem state. Main gap is lack of explicit signal handlers for graceful shutdown logging.
top_issues: No SIGTERM/SIGINT handler to update state.json to interrupted phase
No explicit WAL — .jsonl files serve as implicit WAL undocumented
